cards.title: Mangal Pandey
cards.subtitle: The First Spark
cards.description: A sepoy of the 34th Bengal Native Infantry at Barrackpore, Mangal Pandey attacked his British officers on 29 March 1857 in protest against the greased cartridges of the new Enfield rifle. He was hanged on 8 April 1857, and his defiance is widely seen as a prelude to the larger revolt.

cards.title: The Revolt of 1857
cards.subtitle: The First War of Independence
cards.description: Beginning with the mutiny of sepoys at Meerut on 10 May 1857, the uprising spread across northern and central India against the rule of the British East India Company. Though suppressed by 1858, it led to the end of Company rule and the transfer of power to the British Crown.

cards.title: Bahadur Shah Zafar
cards.subtitle: The Last Mughal
cards.description: The last Mughal Emperor, a poet by temperament, was proclaimed the symbolic leader of the 1857 rebels in Delhi. After the revolt's defeat he was tried by the British, exiled to Rangoon, and died there in 1862, ending the Mughal dynasty.

cards.title: Rani Lakshmibai
cards.subtitle: The Queen of Jhansi
cards.description: The Rani of Jhansi resisted the British annexation of her state under the Doctrine of Lapse and led her forces in battle during the 1857 revolt. She died fighting near Gwalior in June 1858 and became an enduring symbol of resistance.

cards.title: Tatya Tope
cards.subtitle: The Guerrilla General
cards.description: A close associate of Nana Saheb, Tatya Tope was among the most able military leaders of the 1857 revolt, waging a prolonged guerrilla campaign across central India. He was captured and executed in April 1859.

cards.title: Nana Saheb
cards.subtitle: The Peshwa's Heir
cards.description: The adopted son of the last Peshwa Baji Rao II, Nana Saheb led the rebellion at Kanpur in 1857 after the British denied him his pension. He disappeared after the revolt's suppression and his ultimate fate remains uncertain.

cards.title: Begum Hazrat Mahal
cards.subtitle: The Begum of Awadh
cards.description: A leader of the 1857 revolt in Awadh, she organized resistance at Lucknow on behalf of her young son after the British annexation of the kingdom. When the rebellion failed she took refuge in Nepal, where she died in 1879.

cards.title: The British Raj
cards.subtitle: Rule from the Crown
cards.description: Following the 1857 revolt, the Government of India Act 1858 transferred control of India from the East India Company to the British Crown, beginning direct rule. Queen Victoria was proclaimed Empress of India in 1877.

cards.title: Raja Ram Mohan Roy
cards.subtitle: Father of the Indian Renaissance
cards.description: A pioneering social and religious reformer, he founded the Brahmo Sabha in 1828 and campaigned against sati and for women's rights and modern education. Though he died in 1833, his reformist legacy shaped the intellectual ground of the freedom movement.

cards.title: Arya Samaj
cards.subtitle: Back to the Vedas
cards.description: Founded by Swami Dayananda Saraswati in 1875, the Arya Samaj called for a return to the Vedas and campaigned against social ills such as caste rigidity and untouchability. Its emphasis on education and self-respect contributed to a rising national consciousness.

cards.title: Dadabhai Naoroji
cards.subtitle: The Grand Old Man of India
cards.description: An early nationalist leader and a founder of the Indian National Congress, Naoroji developed the influential 'Drain of Wealth' theory of colonial exploitation. In 1892 he became the first Indian elected to the British House of Commons.

cards.title: Indian National Congress
cards.subtitle: The Voice of a Nation
cards.description: Founded in 1885 with A. O. Hume's involvement, the Indian National Congress grew into the principal organization of the Indian independence movement. Its annual sessions became the central forum for nationalist debate and demands.

cards.title: Servants of India Society
cards.subtitle: Service to the Nation
cards.description: Founded by Gopal Krishna Gokhale in 1905, the society trained dedicated workers to advance education, social reform, and constitutional progress. It embodied the moderate, service-oriented strand of the early nationalist movement.

cards.title: Gopal Krishna Gokhale
cards.subtitle: The Political Guru
cards.description: A leading moderate of the Indian National Congress, Gokhale advocated constitutional reform and gradual self-government within the British system. He was a mentor to Gandhi, who regarded him as his political teacher.

cards.title: Bal Gangadhar Tilak
cards.subtitle: Lokmanya
cards.description: A leader of the assertive nationalist wing, Tilak declared that 'Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it.' He popularized public Ganesh and Shivaji festivals to build national feeling and edited the newspapers Kesari and Maratha.

cards.title: Kesari and the Nationalist Press
cards.subtitle: Words as Weapons
cards.description: Newspapers such as Tilak's Kesari and Maratha, and later Gandhi's Young India and Harijan, spread nationalist ideas and exposed colonial policies. The press became a powerful instrument of political awakening despite repeated government restrictions.

cards.title: Partition of Bengal
cards.subtitle: The Line That United Bengal
cards.description: In 1905 Viceroy Lord Curzon partitioned Bengal, a move widely seen as an attempt to weaken nationalist sentiment. The intense protest it provoked launched the Swadeshi movement, and the partition was annulled in 1911.

cards.title: Swadeshi
cards.subtitle: Of One's Own Country
cards.description: Sparked by the 1905 partition of Bengal, the Swadeshi movement urged Indians to boycott British goods and use indigenous products. It encouraged native industry, education, and self-reliance as forms of political resistance.

cards.title: Vande Mataram
cards.subtitle: The Song of the Motherland
cards.description: Written by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay and drawn from his novel Anandamath, 'Vande Mataram' became a rallying cry of the freedom movement during the Swadeshi era. It was later adopted as the national song of India.

cards.title: The Surat Split
cards.subtitle: Moderates and Extremists Part
cards.description: At the 1907 Congress session in Surat, the party split between the Moderates, who favoured constitutional methods, and the Extremists, who pressed for more assertive action. The two factions reunited at the Lucknow session in 1916.

cards.title: Lala Lajpat Rai
cards.subtitle: The Lion of Punjab
cards.description: One of the trio known as Lal-Bal-Pal, Lajpat Rai was a prominent leader of the assertive nationalist movement and a social reformer. He was fatally injured in a lathi charge while protesting the Simon Commission in 1928.

cards.title: Bipin Chandra Pal
cards.subtitle: The Voice of Assertion
cards.description: A leading figure of the assertive nationalist wing and part of the Lal-Bal-Pal trio, Pal championed Swadeshi, boycott, and national education. He was a powerful orator and journalist during the Swadeshi era.

cards.title: Anushilan Samiti
cards.subtitle: The Bengal Revolutionaries
cards.description: One of the earliest revolutionary organizations, the Anushilan Samiti operated in Bengal in the early twentieth century, combining physical culture with armed nationalism. Its members were involved in several actions against colonial officials.

cards.title: Ghadar Party
cards.subtitle: Revolt from Overseas
cards.description: Founded in 1913 by Indian emigrants on the west coast of North America, the Ghadar Party aimed to overthrow British rule through armed revolution. Many of its members returned to India during the First World War to organize an uprising.

cards.title: Bhikaji Cama
cards.subtitle: Mother of the Revolution
cards.description: A pioneer of the freedom movement abroad, Bhikaji Cama unfurled an early version of an Indian flag at the International Socialist Congress in Stuttgart in 1907. She campaigned tirelessly for Indian independence from Europe.

cards.title: Annie Besant
cards.subtitle: The Home Rule Crusader
cards.description: An Irish-born theosophist and reformer, Annie Besant launched the Home Rule League in 1916 to demand self-government for India. In 1917 she became the first woman president of the Indian National Congress.

cards.title: Subramania Bharati
cards.subtitle: The Poet of Freedom
cards.description: A Tamil poet and journalist, Bharati composed stirring patriotic verse that called for freedom and social reform, including the emancipation of women. His songs inspired the national movement in southern India.

cards.title: Champaran Satyagraha
cards.subtitle: Gandhi's First Campaign
cards.description: In 1917 Gandhi led his first major satyagraha in India at Champaran, Bihar, supporting indigo cultivators oppressed by the forced tinkathia system. The campaign's success established his method of nonviolent resistance on Indian soil.

cards.title: Mahatma Gandhi
cards.subtitle: The Father of the Nation
cards.description: Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi led the Indian independence movement through the principles of satyagraha and ahimsa, mobilizing millions in mass nonviolent campaigns. He is honoured as the Father of the Nation and was assassinated in January 1948.

cards.title: Satyagraha
cards.subtitle: The Force of Truth
cards.description: Developed by Gandhi, satyagraha is the method of nonviolent resistance based on truth and moral force rather than physical violence. It became the guiding strategy of the Indian freedom movement under his leadership.

cards.title: Ahimsa
cards.subtitle: The Creed of Non-Violence
cards.description: Ahimsa, or non-violence, was the moral foundation of Gandhi's political philosophy and of the mass movements he led. It insisted that the means of struggle must be as pure as the goal of freedom.

cards.title: Kasturba Gandhi
cards.subtitle: Ba
cards.description: The wife and lifelong companion of Mahatma Gandhi, Kasturba took part in numerous satyagraha campaigns and was repeatedly imprisoned. She died in 1944 while detained at the Aga Khan Palace during the Quit India movement.

cards.title: Sabarmati Ashram
cards.subtitle: The Abode of Service
cards.description: Established by Gandhi on the bank of the Sabarmati river near Ahmedabad in 1917, the ashram became a centre of his constructive and political work. The historic Dandi March set out from here in 1930.

cards.title: Rowlatt Satyagraha
cards.subtitle: No Appeal, No Plea, No Lawyer
cards.description: In 1919 the Rowlatt Act granted the government wide powers to detain people without trial, prompting Gandhi to call a nationwide satyagraha. The protest marked the first all-India political agitation under his leadership.

cards.title: Jallianwala Bagh Massacre
cards.subtitle: The Garden of Sorrow
cards.description: On 13 April 1919, troops under Brigadier-General Dyer opened fire on an unarmed crowd gathered in the enclosed Jallianwala Bagh in Amritsar, killing hundreds. The massacre deeply shocked the nation and intensified the demand for freedom.

cards.title: Amritsar
cards.subtitle: City of the Massacre
cards.description: The Punjabi city of Amritsar, home to the Golden Temple, became a focal point of the freedom struggle after the Jallianwala Bagh massacre of 1919. The events there galvanized nationwide protest against colonial rule.

cards.title: Khilafat Movement
cards.subtitle: A Common Cause
cards.description: Launched around 1919 by Indian Muslims to protest the treatment of the Ottoman Caliph after the First World War, the Khilafat movement allied with Gandhi's campaign. It briefly forged a remarkable unity between Hindu and Muslim political action.

cards.title: Non-Cooperation Movement
cards.subtitle: Withdrawing Consent
cards.description: Launched by Gandhi in 1920, the Non-Cooperation Movement called on Indians to boycott British institutions, schools, courts, titles, and goods. It transformed the freedom struggle into a mass movement involving millions.

cards.title: Khadi
cards.subtitle: The Cloth of Freedom
cards.description: Khadi, the handspun and handwoven cloth promoted by Gandhi, became a symbol of self-reliance and resistance to British textile imports. Wearing khadi was both an economic act and a badge of national identity.

cards.title: The Charkha
cards.subtitle: The Spinning Wheel
cards.description: The spinning wheel, or charkha, was central to Gandhi's vision of self-reliance and rural empowerment through hand-spun yarn. It became one of the most recognized symbols of the freedom movement and appeared on early national flags.

cards.title: Chauri Chaura Incident
cards.subtitle: The Movement Halted
cards.description: In February 1922 a mob at Chauri Chaura in the United Provinces set fire to a police station, killing several policemen. Distressed by the violence, Gandhi called off the Non-Cooperation Movement.

cards.title: Motilal Nehru
cards.subtitle: Architect of the Nehru Report
cards.description: A leading lawyer and Congress president, Motilal Nehru co-founded the Swaraj Party and chaired the committee that produced the 1928 Nehru Report on India's constitutional future. He was the father of Jawaharlal Nehru.

cards.title: Kakori Conspiracy
cards.subtitle: The Train Robbery
cards.description: In August 1925 revolutionaries of the Hindustan Republican Association looted a train carrying government money near Kakori to fund their armed struggle. Several participants, including Ram Prasad Bismil and Ashfaqulla Khan, were tried and executed.

cards.title: Ram Prasad Bismil
cards.subtitle: The Poet-Revolutionary
cards.description: A leading figure of the Hindustan Republican Association and a key participant in the Kakori action, Bismil was also a poet associated with the verse 'Sarfaroshi ki Tamanna.' He was executed at Gorakhpur jail in December 1927.

cards.title: Ashfaqulla Khan
cards.subtitle: Comrade of Kakori
cards.description: A revolutionary of the Hindustan Republican Association and a close comrade of Ram Prasad Bismil, Ashfaqulla Khan took part in the Kakori action. He was executed at Faizabad jail in December 1927 and is remembered as a symbol of unity.

cards.title: Hindustan Socialist Republican Association
cards.subtitle: Arms for a Republic
cards.description: Reorganized in 1928 under leaders such as Chandrashekhar Azad and Bhagat Singh, the HSRA sought complete independence through revolutionary means and a socialist republic. Its members carried out several celebrated actions against colonial authority.

cards.title: Simon Commission
cards.subtitle: Simon Go Back
cards.description: Sent in 1928 to review constitutional reforms, the all-British Simon Commission included no Indian members and was met with widespread protests and black-flag demonstrations. The slogan 'Simon Go Back' echoed across the country.

cards.title: Bhagat Singh
cards.subtitle: Shaheed-e-Azam
cards.description: A revolutionary of the HSRA, Bhagat Singh threw a bomb in the Central Legislative Assembly in 1929 to 'make the deaf hear' and was tried for the killing of a police officer. He was executed in Lahore on 23 March 1931 along with Rajguru and Sukhdev.

cards.title: Shivaram Rajguru
cards.subtitle: Marksman of the Revolution
cards.description: A revolutionary associated with the HSRA, Rajguru took part in the action that the group carried out in Lahore in 1928. He was executed alongside Bhagat Singh and Sukhdev on 23 March 1931.

cards.title: Sukhdev Thapar
cards.subtitle: The Quiet Organizer
cards.description: A leading member of the HSRA and a close associate of Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev helped build the revolutionary network in Punjab. He was executed with Bhagat Singh and Rajguru in Lahore on 23 March 1931.

cards.title: Chandrashekhar Azad
cards.subtitle: Never to Be Captured
cards.description: A fearless leader of the HSRA, Azad vowed never to be taken alive by the British. He died in a gun battle at Alfred Park in Allahabad in February 1931.

cards.title: Inquilab Zindabad
cards.subtitle: Long Live the Revolution
cards.description: The slogan 'Inquilab Zindabad,' meaning 'Long Live the Revolution,' became a rallying cry of the freedom struggle, raised most famously by Bhagat Singh and his comrades. It captured the spirit of defiance against colonial rule.

cards.title: Purna Swaraj
cards.subtitle: Complete Independence
cards.description: At the Lahore session of 1929 the Congress declared Purna Swaraj, or complete independence, as its goal. On 26 January 1930 it called for the public observance of Independence Day, a date later chosen for the Republic.

cards.title: Lahore Session 1929
cards.subtitle: The Flag on the Ravi
cards.description: Presided over by Jawaharlal Nehru, the 1929 Lahore session of the Congress adopted the resolution for complete independence. The tricolour was hoisted on the bank of the river Ravi as a pledge of Purna Swaraj.

cards.title: Dandi Salt March
cards.subtitle: A Fistful of Salt
cards.description: In March and April 1930 Gandhi led a 240-mile march from Sabarmati Ashram to the coastal village of Dandi, where he broke the salt law by making salt from seawater. The act launched the Civil Disobedience Movement.

cards.title: Dandi
cards.subtitle: Where the Salt Was Made
cards.description: A coastal village in Gujarat, Dandi was the destination of Gandhi's 1930 march, where he picked up a handful of natural salt in defiance of the salt tax. The site became a landmark of nonviolent resistance.

cards.title: The Salt
cards.subtitle: The People's Right
cards.description: The British monopoly and tax on salt, a daily necessity, made it a powerful symbol of colonial injustice. By making salt from the sea, Indians turned an ordinary substance into an emblem of mass civil disobedience.

cards.title: Civil Disobedience Movement
cards.subtitle: Breaking Unjust Laws
cards.description: Beginning with the Salt March in 1930, the Civil Disobedience Movement involved the deliberate, nonviolent breaking of colonial laws and boycott of British goods. It mobilized people across the country, including large numbers of women.

cards.title: Sarojini Naidu
cards.subtitle: The Nightingale of India
cards.description: A poet and orator, Sarojini Naidu was a prominent leader of the freedom movement and the first Indian woman to become president of the Congress, in 1925. She led the Dharasana salt raid in 1930 and later became Governor of the United Provinces.

cards.title: Round Table Conferences
cards.subtitle: Talks in London
cards.description: Held in London between 1930 and 1932, the three Round Table Conferences discussed constitutional reform for India. Gandhi attended the second conference in 1931 as the sole representative of the Congress.

cards.title: Gandhi-Irwin Pact
cards.subtitle: A Truce Signed
cards.description: Signed in March 1931, the Gandhi-Irwin Pact suspended the Civil Disobedience Movement in exchange for the release of political prisoners and concessions on salt. It paved the way for Gandhi's participation in the Second Round Table Conference.

cards.title: The Tricolour
cards.subtitle: Saffron, White, and Green
cards.description: The national flag evolved through several designs during the freedom struggle before the tricolour of saffron, white, and green with a central wheel was adopted in 1947. It became the foremost emblem of the independent nation.

cards.title: B. R. Ambedkar
cards.subtitle: Architect of the Constitution
cards.description: A jurist, economist, and champion of social justice, Ambedkar led the movement for the rights of the depressed classes and against untouchability. He chaired the drafting committee of the Indian Constitution and became independent India's first Law Minister.

cards.title: Poona Pact
cards.subtitle: An Agreement on Representation
cards.description: Concluded in 1932 between B. R. Ambedkar and Gandhi, the Poona Pact replaced separate electorates for the depressed classes with reserved seats within the general electorate. It followed Gandhi's fast against the Communal Award.

cards.title: Subhas Chandra Bose
cards.subtitle: Netaji
cards.description: A radical nationalist leader and twice president of the Congress, Bose championed complete independence through more assertive means. He later organized the Indian National Army abroad and gave the call 'Give me blood, and I shall give you freedom.'

cards.title: Forward Bloc
cards.subtitle: A New Front
cards.description: Founded by Subhas Chandra Bose in 1939 after he left the Congress presidency, the Forward Bloc sought to unite the more radical elements of the freedom movement. It pressed for uncompromising opposition to British rule.

cards.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
cards.subtitle: Pandit Ji
cards.description: A foremost leader of the Congress and a close associate of Gandhi, Nehru championed complete independence, secularism, and modern development. He delivered the 'Tryst with Destiny' speech and became independent India's first Prime Minister.

cards.title: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel
cards.subtitle: The Iron Man
cards.description: A leading organizer of the Congress, Patel earned the title Sardar after leading the Bardoli Satyagraha of 1928. As independent India's first Deputy Prime Minister and Home Minister, he integrated the princely states into the Union.

cards.title: Maulana Abul Kalam Azad
cards.subtitle: Scholar and Statesman
cards.description: A scholar, journalist, and senior Congress leader, Azad was a strong advocate of Hindu-Muslim unity and a united India. He served as Congress president during the Quit India years and became independent India's first Education Minister.

cards.title: Rajendra Prasad
cards.subtitle: The First President
cards.description: A lawyer and Congress leader who joined Gandhi during the Champaran campaign, Rajendra Prasad presided over the Constituent Assembly. He became the first President of the Republic of India in 1950.

cards.title: C. Rajagopalachari
cards.subtitle: Rajaji
cards.description: A senior Congress statesman and close associate of Gandhi, Rajaji took part in the Salt Satyagraha in the south. He became the first and only Indian Governor-General of independent India before the country became a republic.

cards.title: Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan
cards.subtitle: Frontier Gandhi
cards.description: A Pashtun leader from the North-West Frontier, Ghaffar Khan founded the nonviolent Khudai Khidmatgar movement and was a close ally of Gandhi. His commitment to nonviolence earned him the name Frontier Gandhi.

cards.title: All-India Muslim League
cards.subtitle: A Separate Path
cards.description: Founded in 1906 at Dhaka, the All-India Muslim League came to represent demands for the political interests of Indian Muslims. In 1940 it adopted the Lahore Resolution calling for separate states, leading toward the creation of Pakistan.

cards.title: Muhammad Ali Jinnah
cards.subtitle: Quaid-e-Azam
cards.description: A barrister and leader of the All-India Muslim League, Jinnah was once hailed as an ambassador of Hindu-Muslim unity before championing the demand for Pakistan. He became the first Governor-General of Pakistan in 1947.

cards.title: V. D. Savarkar
cards.subtitle: The Cellular Prisoner
cards.description: A revolutionary, writer, and political leader, Savarkar was transported to the Cellular Jail in the Andamans for his anti-colonial activities. He authored a history of the 1857 revolt and later articulated the political philosophy of Hindutva.

cards.title: Cellular Jail
cards.subtitle: Kala Pani
cards.description: Built by the British on the Andaman Islands, the Cellular Jail, known as Kala Pani, was used to imprison political prisoners in solitary confinement. Many freedom fighters endured harsh punishment within its radiating wings.

cards.title: Birsa Munda
cards.subtitle: Dharti Aaba
cards.description: A young tribal leader of the Munda people, Birsa Munda led the Ulgulan uprising against colonial land policies and exploitation in the Chhotanagpur region in the 1890s. Revered as Dharti Aaba, he died in jail in 1900.

cards.title: Alluri Sitarama Raju
cards.subtitle: Manyam Veerudu
cards.description: Alluri Sitarama Raju led the Rampa Rebellion of 1922-1924, mobilizing tribal communities of the Eastern Ghats against colonial forest laws. Known as Manyam Veerudu, the Hero of the Jungle, he was captured and killed in 1924.

cards.title: Surya Sen
cards.subtitle: Master-da
cards.description: A schoolteacher and revolutionary, Surya Sen led the Chittagong Armoury Raid of 1930, in which revolutionaries seized colonial arms depots. Known as Master-da, he was captured and executed in 1934.

cards.title: Matangini Hazra
cards.subtitle: Gandhi Buri
cards.description: An elderly peasant woman of Bengal affectionately called Gandhi Buri, Matangini Hazra led a procession during the Quit India movement in 1942. She was shot while carrying the national flag and is remembered for her courage.

cards.title: India and the Second World War
cards.subtitle: Drawn into the Conflict
cards.description: In 1939 the British declared India a participant in the Second World War without consulting Indian leaders, prompting Congress ministries to resign in protest. The wartime crisis sharpened demands for immediate independence.

cards.title: Cripps Mission
cards.subtitle: A Post-Dated Cheque
cards.description: Sent in 1942, the Cripps Mission offered India dominion status after the war in exchange for wartime cooperation. The Congress rejected the proposals, with the offer famously dismissed as a 'post-dated cheque.'

cards.title: Quit India Movement
cards.subtitle: Do or Die
cards.description: Launched by Gandhi in August 1942 with the call 'Do or Die,' the Quit India Movement demanded an immediate end to British rule. The arrest of leaders sparked a widespread, largely spontaneous mass uprising across the country.

cards.title: August Kranti Maidan
cards.subtitle: Cradle of Quit India
cards.description: Formerly known as Gowalia Tank Maidan in Bombay, this ground was where Gandhi delivered the Quit India call on 8 August 1942. It was renamed August Kranti Maidan to mark the launch of the movement.

cards.title: Aruna Asaf Ali
cards.subtitle: Heroine of Quit India
cards.description: A leader of the Quit India movement, Aruna Asaf Ali hoisted the national flag at Gowalia Tank in Bombay in 1942 and went underground to direct the agitation. She is remembered as the Grand Old Lady of the freedom movement.

cards.title: Usha Mehta
cards.subtitle: Voice of the Secret Radio
cards.description: A young activist during the Quit India movement, Usha Mehta helped operate the Congress Radio, a secret underground station that broadcast nationalist messages in 1942. She was arrested and imprisoned for her role.

cards.title: Indian National Army
cards.subtitle: Azad Hind Fauj
cards.description: The Indian National Army, or Azad Hind Fauj, was organized in Southeast Asia to fight for India's independence, and was revitalized under Subhas Chandra Bose in 1943. Its struggle inspired strong nationalist sentiment at home.

cards.title: Rani of Jhansi Regiment
cards.subtitle: Women in Arms
cards.description: The Rani of Jhansi Regiment was an all-women combat unit of the Indian National Army, formed in 1943 and led by Captain Lakshmi Sahgal. It was among the earliest organized women's military formations of its kind in Asia.

cards.title: The INA Trials
cards.subtitle: The Red Fort Trials
cards.description: In 1945 and 1946 the British court-martialled officers of the Indian National Army at the Red Fort in Delhi. The trials provoked huge public sympathy and nationwide protests in support of the accused.

cards.title: The Red Fort
cards.subtitle: Where the Flag Rises
cards.description: The Mughal-era Red Fort in Delhi was the setting of the INA trials and a powerful symbol of sovereignty. From its ramparts the Prime Minister hoists the national flag each Independence Day.

cards.title: Royal Indian Navy Mutiny
cards.subtitle: The Sailors Rise
cards.description: In February 1946 sailors of the Royal Indian Navy at Bombay and other ports rose in revolt over conditions and national grievances. The mutiny signalled the weakening grip of colonial authority over the armed services.

cards.title: Cabinet Mission
cards.subtitle: A Plan for Transfer
cards.description: Sent in 1946, the British Cabinet Mission proposed a framework for transferring power and forming a constitution-making body. Its plan was eventually overtaken by the demand for partition.

cards.title: The Mountbatten Plan
cards.subtitle: The Decision to Divide
cards.description: Announced in June 1947 by the last Viceroy, Lord Mountbatten, the plan set out the partition of British India into two dominions and an accelerated transfer of power. It led to the Indian Independence Act passed by the British Parliament.

cards.title: Independence
cards.subtitle: Tryst with Destiny
cards.description: At midnight on 15 August 1947 India became independent, with Jawaharlal Nehru proclaiming a 'tryst with destiny.' The national flag was raised as British rule formally came to an end.

cards.title: Partition of India
cards.subtitle: The Great Divide
cards.description: The independence of 1947 was accompanied by the partition of British India into India and Pakistan. The division triggered one of the largest mass migrations in history and widespread communal violence.

cards.title: The Radcliffe Line
cards.subtitle: A Border Drawn in Weeks
cards.description: The boundary dividing India and Pakistan was demarcated by a commission chaired by Sir Cyril Radcliffe and published shortly after independence. The hastily drawn line cut through the provinces of Punjab and Bengal.

cards.title: Lahore
cards.subtitle: City of Many Histories
cards.description: An ancient city and cultural centre, Lahore was the site of the 1929 Congress session that declared complete independence and the 1940 Muslim League resolution. At Partition in 1947 it became part of Pakistan.

cards.title: Integration of the Princely States
cards.subtitle: One Union
cards.description: After independence the hundreds of princely states were persuaded or pressed to accede to the new nation, a task led by Sardar Patel and V. P. Menon. Their integration welded a fragmented subcontinent into a single Union.

cards.title: The Constituent Assembly
cards.subtitle: We, the People
cards.description: Convened in December 1946, the Constituent Assembly framed the Constitution of independent India over nearly three years of deliberation. It was presided over by Rajendra Prasad, with B. R. Ambedkar chairing the drafting committee.

cards.title: The Constitution of India
cards.subtitle: The Republic Is Born
cards.description: Adopted on 26 November 1949 and brought into force on 26 January 1950, the Constitution established India as a sovereign democratic republic. The date of its commencement is celebrated each year as Republic Day.
